summary: A 67-year-old man with moderate-to-bothersome lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) — nocturia 3x, weak stream, hesitancy, IPSS ~14 — and a normal PSA of 1.6 asks his urologist's nurse about medical management options for BPH. The held-out answer must reflect the AUA 2024 BPH guideline framework — alpha-blockers (tamsulosin, alfuzosin, silodosin) as first-line for symptomatic BPH with rapid onset, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride) for men with prostate >30-40 cc with delayed onset (3-6 months), combination therapy supported by MTOPS / CombAT, the role of phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (tadalafil 5 mg daily), and the side-effect profiles (orthostasis with alpha-blockers, sexual side effects with 5-ARI).

messages.content: I'm 67, and I'm up to pee 3 times a night, with a weak stream and lots of starting and stopping. PSA is 1.6, my urologist says no cancer signs. He brought up tamsulosin or finasteride. Which should I take, or both?

do_NOT: describe finasteride as having immediate symptom relief
describe alpha-blockers as having no side effects
skip the prostate-volume context for 5-ARI choice
skip the orthostasis / first-dose effect for alpha-blockers
describe medical therapy as superior to lifestyle / surgical referral for all patients without context

expected_sources_referenced: AUA / Urology Care Foundation Guideline on Management of Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia — Sandhu et al., J Urol 2024
MTOPS — McConnell et al., NEJM 2003 (doxazosin + finasteride combination)
CombAT — Roehrborn et al., Eur Urol 2010 (dutasteride + tamsulosin)
Porst et al., J Urol 2013 — tadalafil for LUTS
